coredata.dc:title: Study on extinguishing performance and suppression mechanism of thermal runaway combustible gas flame of lithium battery by NH4H2PO4 water mist
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Haoyang, Li
Jian, Lu
Jianhao, Wang
Liping, Yang
coredata.dc:description: As the proportion of fires caused by new energy vehicles in road traffic accidents continues to rise, research on the suppression of lithium battery fires has become particularly important. The flames resulting from thermal runaway of batteries primarily originate from the combustion of gases released during the thermal runaway process. Therefore, effectively suppressing the thermal runaway flames of lithium batteries not only requires reducing the battery temperature but also necessitates the effective suppression and extinguishment of combustive gases that escape during thermal runaway. Given that flammable gases such as H2 and CO released during the thermal runaway of lithium iron phosphate batteries account for more than 95 % of total products, this study innovatively constructs a small-scale flame simulation system using an ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) aqueous solution as a firefighting agent, focusing on exploring its extinguishing efficiency and reaction kinetics mechanism. Using a self-built cup burner experimental system, this study examines the minimum extinguishing concentration (MEC) of fogging agents at different concentration gradients (1 %, 2 %, 5 %, and saturation state). The saturated solution exhibits excellent performance with an MEC of 8.21 %, significantly outperforming traditional extinguishing agents such as nitrogen (11.04 %), carbon dioxide (10.37 %), and perfluorohexane (9.34 %). At the microscopic reaction mechanism level, a comprehensive kinetic model (Overall-Mech) containing 1120 elementary reactions was constructed using Chemkin software, identifying four key suppression pathways. The study reveals that phosphorus-containing active substances significantly deplete H/OH radical concentrations through the dual suppression cycle formation of HOPO↔PO2 and HOPO2↔PO2, reducing the overall reaction rate. In the macro extinguishing experiments, the saturated fine water mist system realized fire extinguishment within 3–5 s, and the battery box temperature decreased by 89 % during the subsequent 10 min of constant temperature control, demonstrating both rapid extinguishing and prolonged thermal control protective characteristics. This research provides a technically valuable solution with both theoretical depth and practical applicability for addressing thermal runaway fires in lithium batteries of new energy vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH4H2PO4 fine water mist
Lithium iron phosphate battery
Thermal runaway gas
Cup-burner
Minimum extinguishing concentration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal runaway gas product collection systems
9306 atomizer fine water mist particle size and force analysis
Experimental study on NH4H2PO4 fine water mist extinguishing lithium battery thermal runaway flames
Analysis of thermal runaway gas products
Study on the chemical reaction mechanism of NH4H2PO4 fine water mist for quenching thermal runaway gas flame
Analysis of extinguishing lithium battery thermal runaway flames with NH4H2PO4 fine water mist

Additionally, the project has also been supported by the Innovative Team Project of Ji'nan Government ( 202333036 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of full-scale fire extinguishing experimental device.
Flame pattern of thermally runaway gases as a function of increasing agent type and additive volume.
Sensitivity analysis for different NH4H2PO4 concentrations.
Yields of PO2 in four basic reactions.
The thermal runaway combustion of the battery without adding fire extinguishing agent.
Extinguishing process of NH4H2PO4 fine water mist on battery thermal runaway battery fire.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: I1 is the 1 h rate discharge current, and its value is equal to the rated capacity of the battery.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fine water mist particle size under different air flow conditions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: D10: 10 % by volume of all particles smaller than this size.
D50: 50 % by volume of particles smaller than this size;
D90: 90 % of all particles smaller than this size;

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 100 % SOC thermal runaway gas product fractions.
Main product components of thermal runaway gas of 100 % SOC lithium iron phosphate battery.
Atomization amount of each mass fraction of NH4H2PO4 fine water mist reaching the Cup-burner cup at each PSI value of the 9306 three-channel.
Number of occurrences of PSI values for ten experiments to extinguish thermal runaway gas flame.
Minimum extinguishing concentrations of various extinguishing agents for lithium iron phosphate thermal runaway gas flames.

head.ce:title: Study on extinguishing performance and suppression mechanism of thermal runaway combustible gas flame of lithium battery by NH4H2PO4 water mist

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Transportation and Logistics Engineering, ShanDong JiaoTong University, Jinan, 250357, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Transportation and Logistics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Transportation and logistics Engineering, ShanDong JiaoTong University, Jinan, 250357, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Jiao Tong University Transportation Research Center, Shanghai, 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Jiao Tong University Transportation Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Jiao Tong University Transportation Research Center, Shanghai, 200240, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the proportion of fires caused by new energy vehicles in road traffic accidents continues to rise, research on the suppression of lithium battery fires has become particularly important. The flames resulting from thermal runaway of batteries primarily originate from the combustion of gases released during the thermal runaway process. Therefore, effectively suppressing the thermal runaway flames of lithium batteries not only requires reducing the battery temperature but also necessitates the effective suppression and extinguishment of combustive gases that escape during thermal runaway. Given that flammable gases such as H2 and CO released during the thermal runaway of lithium iron phosphate batteries account for more than 95 % of total products, this study innovatively constructs a small-scale flame simulation system using an ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) aqueous solution as a firefighting agent, focusing on exploring its extinguishing efficiency and reaction kinetics mechanism.

Using a self-built cup burner experimental system, this study examines the minimum extinguishing concentration (MEC) of fogging agents at different concentration gradients (1 %, 2 %, 5 %, and saturation state). The saturated solution exhibits excellent performance with an MEC of 8.21 %, significantly outperforming traditional extinguishing agents such as nitrogen (11.04 %), carbon dioxide (10.37 %), and perfluorohexane (9.34 %). At the microscopic reaction mechanism level, a comprehensive kinetic model (Overall-Mech) containing 1120 elementary reactions was constructed using Chemkin software, identifying four key suppression pathways. The study reveals that phosphorus-containing active substances significantly deplete H/OH radical concentrations through the dual suppression cycle formation of HOPO↔PO2 and HOPO2↔PO2, reducing the overall reaction rate.

In the macro extinguishing experiments, the saturated fine water mist system realized fire extinguishment within 3–5 s, and the battery box temperature decreased by 89 % during the subsequent 10 min of constant temperature control, demonstrating both rapid extinguishing and prolonged thermal control protective characteristics. This research provides a technically valuable solution with both theoretical depth and practical applicability for addressing thermal runaway fires in lithium batteries of new energy vehicles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the large-scale application and technological iteration of new energy vehicles, scientific evaluation of their operational safety and risk prevention has become an important topic in the field of traffic engineering. In the safety study of power battery systems, particular attention should be paid to the thermal runaway phenomenon of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) under mechanical abuse conditions and the mechanisms of secondary disasters. When a vehicle suffers a collision or mechanical damage, the instability of the internal electrochemical system of LIBs can lead to a chain exothermic reaction, which, in the absence of effective thermal management measures, can easily trigger intense combustion or explosive disasters [1].

In terms of LIB thermal runaway protection strategies, research in academia mainly focuses on improving intrinsic safety and advancing fire suppression technologies. In the domain of intrinsic battery safety, material modification (such as the development of solid-state electrolytes) and structural optimization (like modular thermal barrier designs) have been proven to delay the thermal runaway process, though they have not yet become a comprehensive solution to thermal disasters [2,3]. At the level of fire suppression technologies, comparative experiments by the Federal Aviation Administration in the United States have shown that traditional halon fire extinguishers have a suppression efficiency of only 32.7 ± 5.4 % for LIB fires, whereas water-based fire suppression systems demonstrate superior cooling efficacy (fire suppression efficiency increases to 68.9 ± 3.1 %) [4]. This has promoted water mist (WM) technology as a cutting-edge direction in LIB fire prevention research.

The water mist fire suppression system achieves fire inhibition through a dual mechanism: firstly, the rapid cooling effect brought by the phase change and heat absorption of droplets, and secondly, the dilution of oxygen concentration by steam [5,6]. Experimental studies have shown that water mist can produce a decay rate of ≥45 °C/s in the surface temperature gradient of LIBs, and it exhibits superior flame quenching efficiency compared to gaseous fire extinguishers such as Novec 1230 and heptafluoropropane (re-ignition probability reduced by 82 %) [7,8]. Notably, pressure parameters significantly affect the extinguishing efficacy, with high-pressure water mist (>8 MPa) reducing the flame spread rate of large-capacity battery modules (>100 kWh) by 76.3 %, attributed to enhanced droplet permeability and heat exchange area [9].

Numerous studies indicate that water mist technology has specific effects on inhibiting thermal runaway and fire spread; however, there are certain limitations to its application. Experiments by He Yuanhua et al. discovered that the cooling efficiency of water mist is influenced by ambient pressure, and releasing water mist during the thermal runaway stage of lithium batteries can significantly reduce CO2 concentration, but it needs to be optimized with a ventilation system to improve the suppressive effect [10]. Jiang Naiwen's research further verified the applicability of water mist in aviation transport scenarios, where adjusting parameters such as mist flow rate, spray velocity, and mist cone angle effectively suppresses lithium battery fires of different capacities and arrangements, indicating that parameter optimization is crucial to fire suppression efficiency [11].Zhen Lou et al. [12,13] conducted a 1:1 simulation using FDS software to study the gas generation patterns during the thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage cabins under different parameters of water mist spraying. The research found a positive correlation between the suppression effect on gas generation and the flow rate of the fine water mist, the spray cone angle, and the nozzle flow rate, while showing a negative correlation with the droplet size. Furthermore, it was observed that fine water mist exhibited a significant suppressive effect on gas generation.

Research by Zhang Qingsong's team revealed the association between the cooling effect of water mist and the temperature rise rate of batteries; experiments showed a nonlinear decline in cooling efficiency of water mist with increasing temperature rise rates, suggesting the need to combine other fireFighting methods in high temperature rise rate scenarios [14]. Comparative experiments by Xu et al. confirmed that water mist's cooling effect on lithium-ion battery fires is superior to other fire extinguishers, especially in suppressing combustible gas combustion and jet flames, though its single application still carries a risk of re-ignition [15]. Zhang et al., through fire simulation experiments on single-cell and multi-cell modules, found that relying solely on water mist cannot completely extinguish lithium battery fires, requiring combination with other suppression technologies (like aerosol fire extinguishers or inert gases) to achieve long-term prevention and control [16].

In summary, given that powder and gas extinguishing agents are ineffective in preventing the re-ignition of lithium batteries, while singular aqueous extinguishing agents possess significant cooling advantages [17–19], the thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries involves electrolyte decomposition (releasing flammable gases such as H2, CO, CH4) along with redox reactions of the cathode materials. This makes it challenging for mere physical cooling to completely interrupt the chain reaction of combustion [20]. Therefore, a synergistic strategy involving chemical additives has become a necessary research direction. By introducing surfactants (such as sodium dodecyl sulfate), the surface tension of the droplets can be reduced to 28.5 mN/m, enhancing the uniformity of the mist coverage [21]. Meanwhile, chemical additives (such as ammonium phosphate salts) can effectively inhibit combustion reaction kinetics through a free radical capture mechanism [22].

Among various chemical additives, ammonium phosphate salts exhibit multidimensional advantages: their conductivity (<10 μS/cm) is reduced by 2 orders of magnitude compared to potassium salts, significantly lowering the risk of electrical short circuits; a passivation layer of Li3PO4 with a thickness of about 12.7 nm formed by reaction with lithium metal can effectively block ion migration between electrodes; the water solubility of extinguishing residues is increased by 67 % compared to carbonates, facilitating equipment cleaning and maintenance. Based on this, this study selects ammonium phosphate salt-modified water mist as the experimental medium, focusing on its inhibition mechanism for lithium iron phosphate battery (LiFePO4) thermal runaway gas flames [23–26].

In previous studies [27], it was found that the gases expelled during the thermal runaway of lithium batteries carry particulate matter. These solid particles absorb the heat released from the battery and, over time, propagate thermal radiation to the battery surface, increasing the risk associated with thermal runaway. However, current combustion experiments of lithium batteries exhibit significant randomness, making it crucial to conduct repeat experiments to obtain consistent data for integrated research. Furthermore, based on earlier studies [28,29], the combustion and fire hazard of lithium batteries following thermal runaway are primarily determined by their gaseous products. Therefore, this paper will focus solely on the gaseous components of the products released during thermal runaway and establish a purely gas-phase flame combustion model based on these components as fuel. This approach not only ensures the reproducibility of experimental research but also simplifies the combustion chemical reaction kinetics mechanism in subsequent mechanism studies, thereby reducing the computational complexity of numerical simulations.

Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries have become a key component in the power systems of electric buses due to their long cycle life and outstanding economic performance. In this study, a 24Ah aluminum-shell prismatic LiFePO4 battery produced by a certain company was selected as the experimental subject. The single cells of this model are encapsulated in aluminum alloy prismatic hard shells, and the module consists of 28 cells connected in series. The entire vehicle's battery system contains 8–12 battery packs, each integrated with 9 modules. The battery modules adopt a top terminal layout design and are equipped with metal film pressure relief valves as pressure release devices. The technical parameters of the battery are shown in Table 1 .

State of Charge (SOC) serves as a crucial parameter in representing the energy storage level of lithium-ion batteries, with its value directly affecting the thermal stability of the battery system. Electrochemical analysis indicates that during charging, lithium ions are extracted from the cathode and migrate to the anode via the electrolyte. An increase in SOC signifies a higher lithium intercalation in the anode. Experimental data show that the exothermic reaction of lithiated graphite anodes with the electrolyte in high-temperature environments has a significant positive correlation. When SOC reaches 100 %, the thermodynamic instability of the battery system peaks, and the probability and intensity of thermal runaway trigger an exponential growth pattern. Based on this thermal safety mechanism, this study selects fully charged state batteries for investigating the characteristics of gas generation during thermal runaway, to obtain key safety parameters under extreme conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal runaway gas product collection systems
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous experience and the experimental requirements of this study, a stainless steel, high-pressure-resistant experimental apparatus for collecting thermal runaway gas from lithium-ion batteries was designed and fabricated, as shown in Fig. 1 .

The experimental device is primarily constructed from austenitic stainless steel, featuring a cylindrical structure with an inner diameter of 21 cm and a length of 34 cm. The tank wall has a thickness of 10 mm, allowing it to withstand a working pressure of 10 MPa. The sealing system is conFigured according to high-pressure vessel standards, with ASME B16.5 standard flanges connecting both ends, complemented by metal-wound gaskets to achieve high-pressure sealing.
The safety protection system includes both rupture disc devices and a spring-loaded safety valve for dual protection, with the safety valve set to trigger at a pressure 1.1 times the design pressure.

Inside the device, a 1000 W armored electric heating module is integrated, with the surface treated to enhance thermal conduction efficiency. The monitoring system consists of a K-type thermocouple (measuring range 0–1200 °C), a piezoresistive pressure sensor (range 0–15 MPa), and a digital vacuum gauge (range -100 kPa–100 kPa). Data is collected in real-time via a paperless recorder, with a sampling frequency of 10 Hz.

The piping system is equipped with four functional interfaces: a vacuum interface connected to a molecular pump unit, a gas collection interface linked to a high-temperature polytetrafluoroethylene gas collection bag, an inert gas line employing a helium replacement system for protection, and an exhaust port fitted with an explosion-proof flame arrestor.

The experimental operation process strictly follows inert environment preparation protocols:
1)
The battery to be tested is fixed onto the heating platform, with a suspended thermocouple positioned at the geometric center area.
2)
A three-stage purification procedure (vacuum extraction to -75 kPa → helium backfill to atmospheric pressure) is performed three times to reduce oxygen concentration below 100 ppm.
3)
The temperature is increased at a gradient rate of 5 °C/min until the thermal runaway characteristic point is triggered (pressure rise rate ≥10 kPa/s).
4)
Once the pressure stabilizes, an aerosol and gas collection system is employed to capture aerosol and gaseous byproducts. The entire process complies with ISO 6469–1:2019 regarding environmental control requirements for battery safety testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 9306 atomizer fine water mist particle size and force analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a TSI 9306 six-nozzle aerosol generator was used to atomize a solution of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4). Based on detailed research in the literature regarding the dynamic characteristics and particle size distribution of aerosol particles produced by similar atomizers, this experiment primarily focused on investigating the effect of additive concentration on the atomization performance.

Experimental data shows that under constant working pressure, the aerosol particles generated from various mass fractions of NH4H2PO4 solution maintained a D50 value ranging from 18 to 22 μm, indicating that variations in the concentration of this inorganic salt additive do not result in statistically significant differences in the particle size output from the atomizer (p > 0.05). Table 2 presents detailed parameters for the particle size distribution of a 5 % mass fraction ammonium dihydrogen phosphate solution, with a characteristic particle size Dv(0.5) of 20.3 μm and a span value of 1.82, which meets the engineering requirements for aerosol fireFighting systems concerning atomized particles.

It is noteworthy that as a flame-retardant additive, ammonium dihydrogen phosphate can decompose at high temperatures to form a polymeric ammonium phosphate barrier layer. This characteristic, coupled with its atomized particle size, influences its fireFighting efficacy.
Fitting error: the calculation error generated when converting the energy spectrum data to the particle size distribution data.

Through calculations, under the experimental conditions of this study, the maximum carrying particle size of the airflow is determined to be 61.1 μm, while the average particle size of the atomized salt solution is approximately 0.58 μm. Therefore, it can be concluded that the atomized salt solution during the experiment can effectively flow with the airflow into the flame zone to participate in reactions.

The diffusion flame cup burner used in this study exhibits significant advantages in simulating flame characteristics: the non-premixed flame it produces closely resembles real-world diffusion flames and possesses enhanced flame stability and resistance to disturbances. This reinforced stability characteristic necessitates the application of higher doses of fire extinguishing agents during extinguishing experiments, resulting in fire extinguishing efficacy parameters that are more reflective of actual fire conditions. This device has been widely adopted in the field of fire engineering, and its testing data has been incorporated into several industry standard technical manuals.

It is important to note that traditional cup burners are primarily suited for gaseous extinguishing agents. For the liquid ammonium dihydrogen phosphate solution mist extinguishing system addressed in this study, the delivery subsystem has been specially modified—achieving stable quantitative transport of the liquid extinguishing agent by constructing a heterogeneous suppression mechanism. The improved device has undergone reliability verification.

The experimental system (Fig. 2 ) includes core components such as the combustion cup, protective hood, precision gas flow control system, TSI 9306 six-nozzle atomization device, waste gas treatment unit, and high-speed imaging recording system. The gas control system employs a co-flow design for fuel and oxidizer, with the extinguishing agent injected into the combustion zone through the oxidizer transport pipeline. The waste gas collection system is equipped with a replaceable filtering device to ensure experimental safety.
The device's main structure references mature designs from research on the suppression of pyrolytic gas flames from lithium-ion batteries, with its validation process detailed in prior literature.

The experiment strictly adheres to GB/T 20,702 standards. After adjusting parameters such as the airflow rate to 40 L/min and stabilizing the flame height at 8 cm, the extinguishing agent is injected using a gradient incremental method (with each single increment not exceeding 3 % of the current concentration) until complete extinction of the flame is achieved. To establish a multidimensional evaluation system, the experimental setup includes control groups with nitrogen, carbon dioxide, and perfluorohexane to verify the extinguishing efficacy of the ammonium dihydrogen phosphate solution through comparative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental study on NH4H2PO4 fine water mist extinguishing lithium battery thermal runaway flames
ce:section.ce:para: This study conducts an in-depth analysis of the inherent differences between the minimum concentration parameters of extinguishing agents measured using a cup burner and those present in real battery fire scenarios. Although the cup burner can simulate diffusion flame characteristics, it significantly differs from actual battery thermal runaway scenarios in terms of fuel supply mechanisms: under experimental conditions, the flame can be instantly extinguished by cutting off the fuel valve. In contrast, during the thermal runaway of a real battery, continuously released flammable gases and the thermal feedback effect from a high-temperature metal shell significantly increase the risk of re-ignition.

To establish a more engineering-relevant fire extinguishing efficacy evaluation system, this research has built an empirical study platform based on an electric bus battery box (dimensions: 1000 mm × 690 mm × 230 mm), Fig. 3 . This battery box is composed of 9 modules, each containing 28 lithium iron phosphate batteries arranged in 4 rows of 7, for a total of 252 batteries. The central module space has been selected as the testing area, where an integrated system with the following functional modules is installed:
1.
Thermal Runaway Induction Module — An electric heating plate is mounted on the wide surface of the individual battery, and a proportional-integral-derivative controller is implemented to achieve temperature rise control with an accuracy of ±1 °C within the range of 0–300 °C (preset heating rate of 20 °C/min).
2.
Mass Monitoring Module — A high-precision electronic balance (resolution: 0.1 g) is housed using heat-resistant isolating materials to record real-time mass changes during the thermal runaway process.
3.
Extinguishing Implementation Module — A nitrogen-driven high-pressure injection system (operating pressure: 2.5 MPa) is conFigured, with an ignition device and extinguishing agent nozzle positioned above the safety valve.
4.
Visualization and Recording Module — A blast-proof observation window is located at the top of the box, in conjunction with a high-speed imaging system to capture the combustion dynamics process.
The entire setup is placed within a closed testing chamber equipped with an explosion-proof smoke exhaust system, ensuring that the experimental environment is safe and controllable.

The experimental process strictly adheres to the GB/T 20,702 standards: after triggering thermal runaway, when the safety valve opens and flammable gas is detected, the ignition device is activated to form a steady-state jet flame. Subsequently, the extinguishing agent injection system is activated, and the exact time for complete flame extinction is recorded while continuously monitoring for re-ignition within a 10-min period.

To verify the repeatability of the experiments, each extinguishing agent parameter is subjected to dual-sample testing, and the actual injection amount is determined using a mass difference method. The temperature monitoring system employs a matrix of K-type thermocouples, focusing on monitoring the temperature evolution characteristics at the center point of the battery (Tc1) and 5 mm above the safety valve opening (Tc2), Fig. 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of thermal runaway gas products
ce:section.ce:para: GC-MS was employed for component analysis of the thermal runaway gases collected in the gas collection bag. To ensure the reliability of the experimental results, the tests were conducted five times. The results of the gas component tests were normalized, and the average of the five experiments was taken to obtain the composition of the thermal runaway gas products from LiFePO4 batteries at 100 % State of Charge (SOC), as shown in Table 3 .

The composition of thermal runaway gases from lithium batteries is complex, with most components accounting for less than 1 %. Among them, H2, CO2, C2H4, CO, CH4, C2H6, and C3H6 together constitute over 95 % of the composition, and their contents are relatively stable. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, minor gas components were disregarded, and only these seven major components were considered. In combustion and explosion-related experiments, this approximation has a minimal impact on the accuracy of the experimental results and is practically meaningful. The main product components are shown in Table 4 .

During the thermal runaway process of lithium-ion batteries, the gas generation mechanism is closely related to the temperature evolution. When the battery temperature exceeds the critical threshold of 120 °C, the electrolyte, binder, and electrode materials undergo thermal decomposition and interactions, leading to the formation of major byproducts such as CO2, CO, and various hydrocarbon gases. Research by Dahn and Li et al. [26,30]. has confirmed that the formation of CO2 is attributed to the oxidation reactions of lithium metal and the oxidation of hydrocarbons, where the constrained oxygen environment (including oxygen released from the cathode and supplied from the surroundings) results in the incomplete oxidation of some hydrocarbons, generating CO. Notably, CO generation follows dual pathways: in addition to the primary reaction pathway, there exists a secondary pathway where trace CO2 embedded in the lithium-intercalated anode is reduced [31].

Regarding the generation mechanism of H2, experimental data indicate that it originates from the reduction reactions between binders such as polyvinylidene fluoride or carboxymethyl cellulose and the lithium-intercalated graphite anode. In terms of hydrocarbon components, scholars such as Spotnitz and Richard have suggested that the formation of C2H4 is related to the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase film, with this process significantly influenced by the participation of lithium metal [32,33]. Additionally, some studies point out that C2H4 may arise from the chemical reaction between the electrolyte ethylene carbonate and metallic lithium.

Short-chain hydrocarbons such as CH4, C2H6, and C3H6 have been confirmed as products of the decomposition of ethyl methyl carbonate and its reactions with lithium. These pathways for the formation of gaseous products highlight the complexity of battery material thermal decomposition reactions and the characteristics of multi-level coupling reactions.

The flow rate of the oxidizer is 40 L min−1. Different fire extinguishing agents such as CO2, N2, perfluorohexane, 1 %, 2 %, 5 % concentrations, and saturated NH4H2PO4 fine mist are added separately to interact with the flame. It can be observed that when different fire extinguishing agents interact with the flame, the color and shape of the flame change accordingly. The variation trend is shown in Fig. 5 .

In the study of the interaction between extinguishing agents and gas flames during thermal runaway of lithium iron phosphate batteries, experimental data reveal the dynamic response characteristics of different extinguishing media. As shown in Fig. 5, CO2, N2, and perfluorohexane can transform the characteristic blue-yellow mixed flame into a uniform light blue. In contrast, the ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist system displays a bright yellow flame, with the concentration of the atomized liquid negatively correlated with flame brightness.

Notably, a unique flame lift phenomenon was observed during the CO2 extinguishing experiments: when the CO2 concentration reached a critical value, the flame flow field was reconstructed, detaching from the mouth of the burner while still maintaining a stable combustion state. To achieve complete extinction, it is necessary to further increase the concentration of the extinguishing agent. This phenomenon may be related to CO2 reducing turbulence intensity and altering flow field velocity distribution, which aligns with the mechanism study on critical extinguishing concentration dynamic regulation found in the literature.

For the evaluation of the extinguishing efficacy of the NH4H2PO4 mist system, the number of activated channels of the 9306 device and the PSI values during extinguishing can be directly recorded. The study found that when three channels are activated, the atomization amount of the NH4H2PO4 solution at various mass fractions is sufficient to extinguish the thermal runaway gas flames. Therefore, this paper only presents the atomization amounts of the NH4H2PO4 solutions at different mass fractions for the scenario with three channels activated, as shown in Table 5 .

Table 6 shows the number of occurrences of PSI values when extinguishing the thermal runaway gas flame in ten experiments for 1 % NH4H2PO4, 2 % NH4H2PO4, 5 % NH4H2PO4, and saturated NH4H2PO4 fine water mist. By calculation, the minimum fire extinguishing concentrations for 1 % NH4H2PO4, 2 % NH4H2PO4, 5 % NH4H2PO4, and saturated NH4H2PO4 are 4.966 L/min, 4.629 L/min, 4.119 L/min, and 3.578 L/min, respectively. It can be observed that as the mass fraction of NH4H2PO4 increases, the minimum fire extinguishing concentration gradually decreases.

To better observe the effectiveness of fine water mist containing NH4H2PO4 in suppressing the thermal runaway gas flame, this study selected N2, CO2, and perfluorohexane as control group experiments. The experimental method is similar to the previous text, and the results are shown in Table 7 .

Comparative experiments indicate that the minimum extinguishing concentrations of traditional inert gases (N2, CO2) are significantly higher than those of perfluorohexane and the ammonium dihydrogen phosphate system. Notably, the extinguishing efficacy of perfluorohexane falls between that of low concentration and saturated ammonium dihydrogen phosphate solution.

It is particularly noteworthy that the ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist system not only maintains excellent extinguishing performance but also demonstrates significant economic advantages. This provides important theoretical support for its engineering application in the field of battery thermal runaway prevention and control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the chemical reaction mechanism of NH4H2PO4 fine water mist for quenching thermal runaway gas flame
ce:section.ce:para: This study is based on the flame structure characteristics and extinguishing conditions of the Cup-burner, proposing a key hypothesis: different gas mixtures exhibit similar transport characteristics in the burner exit region, suggesting that the type of suppressant does not affect the flame morphology in this area. Based on this hypothesis, the ideal stirred reactor model from Chemkin software is selected as the theoretical model for the Cup-burner experiments. This model focuses on the chemical reaction kinetics and the competition mechanism of reactant residence time by ignoring the material transport and mixing effects during the combustion process [34,35].

In addressing the suppression mechanism of the thermal runaway gas flames from lithium iron phosphate batteries, the study constructs a gas-phase interaction model for the NH4H2PO4 mist. By excluding the interference of solid-gas conversion processes on flame suppression, a gas-phase suppression mechanism that incorporates the synergistic effects of NH3 and H3PO4 is established. This comprehensive mechanism, termed Overall-Mech, integrates three key kinetic sub-models: USC-Mech describes the main reaction of thermal runaway gases/air, the Okafor [36] mechanism characterizes the participation of NH3 in the reaction, and the Korobeinichev [37] mechanism elucidates the chemical behavior of H3PO4. The final constructed kinetic system encompasses 197 components and 1685 elementary reactions, making it the most complete gas-phase suppression model for lithium iron phosphate battery thermal runaway to date.

On the kinetic analysis level, the study employs sensitivity analysis to reveal the control mechanisms. Given the orders of magnitude differences in the reaction rate constants within this complex reaction system, numerical simulations identify a dozen key elementary reactions primarily responsible for flame suppression across a gradient of NH4H2PO4 concentrations. The simulation conditions are set to standard environmental parameters (298 K, 1atm, 0 % relative humidity) to ensure the comparability of experimental data with real fire scenarios. The results are visually presented in Fig. 6 , showing the kinetic response profiles of flame suppression effects from different concentrations of the mist, providing a theoretical basis for optimizing extinguishing agent formulations.

Fig. 6 illustrates the impact of different concentrations of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist on key elementary reactions of thermal runaway gas flames, based on the Overall-Mech mechanism model, and presents the distribution of normalized sensitivity coefficients. The study identifies four elementary reactions that are significantly correlated with flame suppression effects: R1092 PO2+OH + M=HOPO2+M、R1098 HOPO2+H=PO2+H2O、R1116 PO2+H + M=HOPO + M、R1120 HOPO + OH=PO2+H2O, These phosphorus-containing elementary reactions exert a suppressive effect on the flame by modulating reaction activity, with their sensitivity coefficients exhibiting a nonlinear characteristic that initially increases and then decreases as the concentration of the extinguishing agent rises.

In terms of combustion-promoting effects, the elementary reaction R1 H + O2=O + OH displays the strongest positive sensitivity coefficient, with its impact continuing to amplify as the concentration of the extinguishing agent increases. Notably, the reactions R1092, R1098, R1116, and R1120, which involve phosphorus-containing substances, exhibit a dual role in regulating the overall reaction rate: on one hand, they reduce reaction activity by accelerating the conversion of phosphorus-based intermediates, while on the other hand, their kinetic influence demonstrates dynamic evolution characteristics with varying concentrations.

To further dissect the microscopic mechanisms of the reaction pathways, the study employs the reaction yield (ROP) analysis method for quantitative characterization of key substances. As shown in Fig. 7 , positive ROP values indicate the net generation contribution of specific substances, while negative values reflect their consumption processes. This method effectively reveals the competitive mechanisms between phosphorus-based suppression reactions and conventional combustion reactions at the substance transformation level, providing microscopic experimental evidence for understanding the dynamic suppression effects across concentrations of the extinguishing agent.

As shown in Fig. 7, the reactions R1120(HOPO + OH→PO2+H2O) and R1098(HOPO2+H→PO2+H2O) jointly constitute the primary pathway for the generation of PO2, while R1116(PO2+H + M→HOPO + M) and R1092(PO2+OH + M→HOPO2+M) dominate its consumption process. It is noteworthy that as the concentration of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist increases, both the absolute rates of generation and consumption of PO2 exhibit a significant enhancement trend.

Specifically, a dynamic cyclical mechanism forms between R1120 and R1116, creating a cycle of HOPO↔PO2, while R1092 and R1098 establish a catalytic conversion pathway of HOPO2↔PO2. These two cyclical systems effectively promote the complex reactions of H and OH free radicals to produce H2O, leading to a reduction in the concentration levels of key chain carriers in the flame by approximately 40–60 %. This results in a dual suppression effect on the combustion chain reaction: it not only accelerates the quenching of active free radicals but also decreases the overall kinetic activity of the reaction system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of extinguishing lithium battery thermal runaway flames with NH4H2PO4 fine water mist
ce:section.ce:para: In the experimentation process and observations of thermal runaway phenomena, this study reveals the dynamic characteristics of thermal runaway in lithium iron phosphate batteries through systematic weighing and thermodynamic monitoring. Experimental data indicate that over five repeated tests, the average total weight loss of the individual cells reached 109.8 g, with the mass loss exhibiting significant phase characteristics.

In detail, the first thermal runaway was completed instantaneously with a weight loss of 54.9 g (accounting for 50 % of the total weight loss). This was followed by a secondary runaway event occurring after an interval of 290 s, during which a sustained weight loss of 45.2 g took place over a period of 110 s. Additionally, there was a transitional period marked by a slow weight loss of 9.7 g. These results confirm the chain reaction characteristics of the thermal runaway process.

Fig. 8 illustrates the typical evolution of combustion behavior under the thermal runaway process of lithium iron phosphate batteries without the application of extinguishing agents. The experiments show that when the heating plate continuously acts on the battery, thermal accumulation triggers internal chain exothermic reactions, leading to the continuous vaporization of the electrolyte. Under thermodynamic coupling, the aluminum shell of the battery undergoes plastic deformation, forming localized bulges. Once the pressure exceeds the capacity of the safety valve, a large amount of gas and white mist is expelled from the damaged safety valve (Fig. 8a).

As the internal pressure surpasses the safety valve threshold, high-pressure gas carries the electrolyte aerosol, breaking through the pressure relief structure and forming a combustible mixture cloud. The active ignition triggers the explosion of the mixture cloud, generating instantaneous shock waves and fireball phenomena (Fig. 8b). The combustion process exhibits typical diffusion flame characteristics, with a continuous jet flame forming at the safety valve outlet (Fig. 8c). The shape of the flame is modulated by the relief rate, presenting periodic pulsations. As the electrolyte is consumed, the combustion intensity shows an exponential decay trend (Fig. 8d).

Notably, after an initial pressure relief of 290 s, a secondary thermal runaway stage occurs (Fig. 8e). The mass loss curve indicates the activation of side reactions at this point, leading to the secondary release of combustible gases. Eventually, the combustion enters a decay phase, with the flame intensity gradually decreasing until it completely extinguishes (Fig. 8f), lasting approximately 110 s. This process reveals that the thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries exhibits multi-stage, nonlinear dynamic characteristics, with two distinct energy release events corresponding to the decomposition reactions of different material components.

Fig. 9
illustrates the suppression effects during the thermal runaway and combustion process of lithium iron phosphate batteries under the intervention of extinguishing agents. Experimental data indicate that under continuous thermal loading from the heating plate, the thermal accumulation within the battery causes the electrolyte to vaporize, ultimately leading to the failure of the safety valve structure and the release of high concentrations of combustible aerosols (Fig. 9a) (see Fig. 10).

Once the aerosol is ignited (Fig. 9b), a sustained jet flame forms at the safety valve (Fig. 9c). At this moment, ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist is precisely released through a high-pressure atomization system (Fig. 9d), achieving rapid extinguishment of the open flame within 3 s (Fig. 9e). Continuous monitoring shows that no re-ignition occurs within 10 min post-extinguishment, and inspections of the battery module inside the enclosure confirm that no structural damage has occurred (Fig. 9f).

Repetitive experiments demonstrate that this extinguishing scheme exhibits stable efficacy, successfully achieving flame suppression within 3–5 s under varying loading amounts of extinguishing agent (ranging from 0.5 to 1.2 L). Furthermore, the distribution of smoke particulate concentration does not exhibit dosage-dependent characteristics.

Notably, the micron-sized droplets generated by the extinguishing agent form a physical barrier in the gas phase. This barrier effectively interrupts the cascading reaction pathways of thermal runaway through a dual mechanism: the heat absorption and cooling effect, alongside the termination of chemical radical chains. As a result, the extinguishing agent significantly mitigates the risks associated with thermal runaway in lithium iron phosphate batteries.

Temperature is a key thermodynamic parameter for characterizing the thermal runaway evolution of lithium-ion batteries and the efficacy of extinguishing agents. Its dynamic changes directly affect the assessment of thermal propagation risks and re-ignition probabilities. In the experiments, a thermocouple array was arranged on the battery surface and at the safety valve outlet (Tc1) to monitor the evolution of temperature fields in real time, revealing the crucial role of the extinguishing agent's cooling performance in suppressing secondary thermal runaway.

Monitoring data (Fig. 10 (1)) indicates that the surface temperature of the battery exhibits multi-stage transition characteristics due to the coupling effects of external heating and internal exothermic reactions. During the initial thermal runaway phase, the ejection of high-temperature gases leads to a temporary surface temperature drop of about 8–12 %. This is followed by a violent reaction between lithium carbonate and electrolyte/Polyvinylidene fluoride, which triggers the decomposition of electrode materials and accelerates the kinetics of reactions due to the active oxygen released, eventually leading to a secondary thermal runaway that peaks at a temperature of 394 °C.

The intervention of different dosages of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate mist significantly alters the thermal equilibrium state due to its high latent heat of vaporization (approximately 2257 kJ/kg) and thermal capacity. Specifically, doses of 0.25 kg, 0.50 kg, and 0.75 kg of the extinguishing agent suppress the peak temperature to 339 °C (a reduction of 13.9 %), 298 °C (24.4 %), and 282 °C (28.1 %), respectively, with the temperature reduction gradient showing a positive correlation with the dosage of the extinguishing agent. It is noteworthy that the temperature curve after extinguishing exhibits a typical “rebound peak,” corresponding to the secondary vaporization process of the residual active substances inside the battery. This phenomenon verifies that merely extinguishing the open flame is insufficient to completely halt the electrochemical chain reactions.

Time constant analysis reveals that different dosages of extinguishing agent significantly shorten the thermal relaxation process. The surface temperature of the untreated group (0 kg) takes 2598 s to decay to 60 °C, while groups treated with 0.25 kg, 0.50 kg, and 0.75 kg of extinguishing agent shorten this time to 1976 s (a reduction of 23.9 %), 1633 s (37.1 %), and 1497 s (42.3 %), respectively. This demonstrates that the extinguishing agent accelerates the system's heat dissipation by reducing thermal inertia. This result aligns closely with the thermal runaway propagation model—when the surface temperature gradient (Delta T/Delta x) drops below a critical value of 5.7 °C/cm, the thermal runaway probability for adjacent battery modules decreases to below 5 %, confirming that an adequate amount of extinguishing agent effectively blocks thermal propagation.

A thermocouple (Tc2) was placed 5 mm above the safety valve outlet to monitor temperature evolution. Experimental data show that different dosages of fine mist ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) extinguishing agent have a significant effect on the suppression of thermal runaway. In the absence of extinguishing agents, the initial thermal runaway produced a jet flame that caused the temperature at the monitoring point to instantaneously reach 550 °C. During the flame decay phase, the temperature dropped below 200 °C, but a subsequent thermal runaway resulted in intense combustion that pushed the inner flame temperature beyond 900 °C. Once the flame was completely extinguished, the monitoring point gradually returned to ambient temperature after an extended period of thermal decay.

With the introduction of fine mist NH4H2PO4 extinguishing agent, its unique physicochemical synergistic effects caused the temperature during the flame suppression phase to plummet to approximately 50 °C, near room temperature. During the secondary thermal runaway, the peak temperature recorded at the monitoring point was only around 100 °C, representing an 89 % reduction compared to untreated conditions. After the thermal runaway was terminated, all dosage groups (0.25 kg, 0.50 kg, and 0.75 kg) accelerated system cooling, with the time taken to return to ambient temperature being reduced by 24 %, 37 %, and 42 %, respectively, compared to the control group (2598 s).

The experiments confirm that this extinguishing agent possesses dual control advantages: during the gas-phase combustion stage, its high latent heat of vaporization facilitates rapid cooling and extinguishment; during the solid-phase reaction stage, the decomposition products form an isolation layer that suppresses chain reactions. Dose-response analysis shows that for every 0.25 kg increase in the amount of extinguishing agent, the peak temperature decreases by 55–60 °C, demonstrating a significant dosage effect. Compared to traditional halogenated hydrocarbon extinguishing agents, this system exhibits superior engineering applicability in blocking thermal propagation and preventing re-ignition.

This study successfully developed a thermal runaway gas collection system for lithium iron phosphate power batteries, allowing for the acquisition of thermal runaway gas samples under 100 % state of charge (SOC) conditions. Quantitative analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) revealed that the gas products are primarily composed of seven components, accounting for over 95 % of the total: hydrogen (H2, 25.6 %), carbon dioxide (CO2, 37.9 %), carbon monoxide (CO, 21.5 %), methane (CH4, 6.7 %), ethylene (C2H4, 5.5 %), ethane (C2H6, 1.3 %), and propylene (C3H6, 1.5 %). Among these, the proportion of combustible gases reached 60.6 %, highlighting the inherent high risk associated with lithium battery fires.

Utilizing an improved Cup-burner experimental platform, the extinguishing efficacy of ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) solutions under various atomization parameters was systematically assessed. Experimental data indicated that the minimum extinguishing concentrations (MEC) for NH4H2PO4 solutions with concentrations of 1 %, 2 %, 5 %, and saturated fine mist were 11.04 %, 10.37 %, 9.34 %, and 8.21 %, respectively, demonstrating a significant advantage over traditional gaseous firefighting agents (perfluorohexane: 9.8 %; nitrogen: 32.6 %; carbon dioxide: 28.4 %).

The phosphorus-based fire suppression kinetic model constructed using Chemkin-Pro, which includes 112 species and 589 reactions under the Overall-Mech mechanism, indicated that phosphorus species primarily realize radical quenching through the following key pathways: R1092 PO2+OH + M=HOPO2+M、R1098 HOPO2+H=PO2+H2O、R1116 PO2+H + M=HOPO + M、R1120 HOPO + OH=PO2+H2O. The elementary reactions involving phosphorus species (such as HOPO2, HOPO, and PO2) play a crucial role in reducing the chemical reaction rate; thus, increasing the rates of these elementary reactions will lead to decreased overall reaction activity. Investigation into the yield of PO2 revealed that reactions R1120 and R1116 form a quenching cycle of HOPO↔PO2, while R1092 and R1098 establish another quenching cycle of HOPO2↔PO2. During the formation of these quenching cycles, the concentrations of H and OH radicals are consumed, resulting in a reduction of the combustion reaction rate.

Experimental validation demonstrated that saturated NH4H2PO4 fine mist could extinguish lithium battery flames within 3–5 s and exhibited excellent thermal management properties, with no resurgence observed within 10 min post-extinction. This confirms that the fire suppression system effectively combines rapid chemical inhibition with sustained physical cooling, providing an innovative solution for the prevention and control of lithium battery fires.

Li Haoyang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Lu Jian: Resources, Funding acquisition. Wang Jianhao: Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yang Liping: Validation, Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Study on Extinguishing Performance and Suppression Mechanism of Thermal Runaway Combustible Gas Flame of Lithium Battery by NH4H2PO4 Water Mist”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project has received support from the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Youth Special Projects (Project Number: ZR2024QE203 and ZR2023QE060). Additionally, the project has also been supported by the Innovative Team Project of Ji'nan Government(202333036).
